candidate_problems.text: What is the minimal and operational notion of 'computational slack' that actually predicts phase transitions between persistent and resolved coordination failure across different multi-agent systems? The conjecture names observability, common knowledge generation, experimentation bandwidth, memory, and selection pressure, but leaves open whether these are reducible to a single measurable quantity, a vector of capacities, or a family of domain-specific thresholds. Without this, the conjecture risks being metaphorical rather than explanatory.

How can this computational-lock-in account be distinguished from standard incentive, mechanism-design, and repeated-game explanations, rather than merely redescribing them in informational language? The unresolved tension is whether 'bad incentives' and 'insufficient computational slack' are genuinely different causal structures or whether incentives are themselves just one component of the system's information-processing architecture. Clarifying this boundary would determine whether the conjecture yields novel predictions or interventions.

Under what conditions does increasing system-level information-processing capacity fail to resolve coordination failure, or even worsen it by stabilizing bad equilibria more efficiently? The conjecture emphasizes that more observability, communication, memory, or experimentation can enable escape, but an open question is whether the same capacities can strengthen surveillance, conformity, adversarial adaptation, path dependence, or rapid propagation of misleading signals. This boundary case is crucial for identifying when computational slack is a true lever versus when it just improves the computation of the wrong policy.
